title: Europe Goes Its Own Way
gist: Henke, Marinova, and Knobloch argue Washington has overlooked a real European strategic awakening: collapsing trust in the US, surging defense spending led by Germany, reintroduced conscription, a push to buy European rather than American hardware, and a new 'principled realism' grand strategy, all forming overlapping security clusters that will outlast Trump and permanently alter the transatlantic bond.

themes.theme: Collapse of trust in the US

insights.claim: Only 11 percent of Europeans across 15 surveyed countries now view the United States as an ally, down from 16 percent six months earlier and 22 percent in November 2024, with 25 percent now seeing the US as a rival or adversary.
insights.speaker: Marina Henke, Iren Marinova, and Till Knobloch
insights.so_what: The transatlantic alliance's political foundation is eroding fast; a structural driver of European defense autonomy and 'buy European' demand.
insights.quote: only 11 percent of Europeans across the 15 surveyed countries... viewed the United States as an ally—down sharply from 16 percent six months earlier and 22 percent in November 2024
insights.locator: EUROPE AWAKENS / 'This sense of insecurity is increased'

insights.claim: 77 percent of Europeans see Russia's war in Ukraine as a direct threat to Europe's survival, with majorities in Germany (59 percent), France (50 percent), and the UK (49 percent) naming Russia the top national-security threat.
insights.speaker: Marina Henke, Iren Marinova, and Till Knobloch
insights.so_what: Threat perception has moved from Europe's periphery to its core, sustaining political support for rearmament.
insights.quote: 77 percent of Europeans think that Russia's war in Ukraine represents a direct threat to Europe's survival
insights.locator: EUROPE AWAKENS / 'According to polling conducted for the European Commission'

insights.claim: In 2024 the 27 EU states spent ~$402 billion on defense, far above Russia's ~$160 billion, with Germany now ~a quarter of EU defense spending and the world's fourth-largest military spender, on track for $172 billion (~3.6% of GDP) by 2029, a ~200% rise from 2022.
insights.speaker: Marina Henke, Iren Marinova, and Till Knobloch
insights.so_what: A multi-year European defense capex supercycle, with Germany the anchor; directly bullish for European defense-industrial names.
insights.quote: the 27 EU member states spent approximately $402 billion on defense, far surpassing Russia's military outlays of $160 billion
insights.locator: GOING IT ALONE / 'European defense spending is going through the roof'

insights.claim: Europe is reshoring military-industrial capacity to cut US dependence, with German startups Helsing and Stark Defense competing for billion-euro drone contracts, a German-Ukrainian venture (Quantum Frontline) producing drones near Munich, and Rheinmetall partnering with Leonardo on 1,000+ vehicles and up to 350 Panther KF51 tanks.
insights.speaker: Marina Henke, Iren Marinova, and Till Knobloch
insights.so_what: Names specific beneficiaries and a defense-tech startup wave; the 'buy European' shift redirects spend away from US primes.
insights.locator: GOING IT ALONE / 'To reduce its dependency on U.S. equipment'

insights.claim: Across 15 countries, 47 percent of Europeans now support collective EU borrowing to finance defense (59% Portugal, 56% Denmark, 55% Netherlands), an idea recently politically unthinkable, and majorities favor buying European over US hardware.
insights.speaker: Marina Henke, Iren Marinova, and Till Knobloch
insights.so_what: Mutualized EU defense debt is moving from taboo to mainstream, a potential new sovereign-issuance and procurement regime.
insights.quote: 47 percent of respondents now support collective EU borrowing to finance defense initiatives

insights.claim: Germany's reactivated voluntary military service drew 12,700 enlistees by end-March 2026 (up 13.5%) and 22,700 career applicants (up 20%), putting it on track toward 260,000 active soldiers and 200,000 reservists by the mid-2030s, with Merz aiming to make the Bundeswehr Europe's strongest conventional army.
insights.speaker: Marina Henke, Iren Marinova, and Till Knobloch
insights.so_what: German manpower buildup is materializing faster than skeptics expected, reinforcing the durability of the rearmament thesis.
insights.quote: advancing Chancellor Friedrich Merz's stated goal of making the Bundeswehr once again 'the strongest conventional army in Europe.'
insights.locator: GOING IT ALONE / 'This development puts the German armed forces'

insights.claim: Majorities in France, Germany, and Poland now support reinstating mandatory military service, and Croatia, Lithuania, Latvia, and Sweden have already reintroduced conscription, reversing a 30-year trend toward all-volunteer forces.
insights.speaker: Marina Henke, Iren Marinova, and Till Knobloch
insights.so_what: A continent-wide remilitarization of society, not just budgets, signaling a durable structural shift.
insights.locator: EUROPE AWAKENS / 'Finally, majorities in France, Germany, and Poland'

insights.claim: Merz's 'principled realism' frames the rights-and-rules order as gone and the world as governed by naked power, requiring Germany to reenter hard power while preserving democratic principles, downgrading the US tie from sentimental friendship to pragmatic partnership.
insights.speaker: Marina Henke, Iren Marinova, and Till Knobloch
insights.so_what: The intellectual scaffolding for a more autonomous, harder-edged Europe; a regime change in German strategic posture.
insights.quote: A sentimental friendship will become a pragmatic partnership.
insights.locator: THE ROAD AHEAD / 'Merz has provided the most coherent account'

insights.claim: Because the 27-member EU is unlikely to adopt a joint grand strategy without ceding sovereignty via majority voting, the likeliest path is overlapping security clusters: a UK-led Joint Expeditionary Force of ten northern states and a French-led 'advanced deterrence' nuclear initiative with nine partners.
insights.speaker: Marina Henke, Iren Marinova, and Till Knobloch
insights.so_what: European defense will federate through coalitions, not Brussels institutions; watch the JEF and French nuclear-sharing as the real loci of capability.
insights.quote: The likeliest development, then, is the emergence of overlapping European security institutions.
insights.locator: WHAT COULD GO WRONG? / 'It is therefore unlikely that the EU'

insights.claim: France's National Rally is polling to win next year's presidential election and the AfD polls ~28% in Germany, threatening the cohesion needed for European rearmament even as both parties have softened explicit anti-EU/NATO stances.
insights.speaker: Marina Henke, Iren Marinova, and Till Knobloch
insights.so_what: Euroskeptic populism is the main internal risk to the rearmament thesis; election calendars are key catalysts to monitor.
insights.quote: the Euroskeptic party Alternative for Germany (AfD) has emerged as a major force, polling at around 28 percent nationally
insights.locator: WHAT COULD GO WRONG? / 'Meanwhile, the Euroskeptic party'

insights.claim: Europe should not wait out Trump, because even a future Democratic administration would likely be pulled toward the Indo-Pacific and China, so the transatlantic tie will not revert to its old form regardless of who governs Washington.
insights.speaker: Marina Henke, Iren Marinova, and Till Knobloch
insights.so_what: The shift to European autonomy is structural, not a Trump-era anomaly; positioning around defense should assume permanence.
insights.quote: Nor would Trump's departure necessarily restore the old order.
insights.locator: ACROSS THE WATER / 'It would be a mistake for Europe to simply wait out Trump'

insights.claim: Cracks are already operational: during Operation Epic Fury in February, Spain barred US warplanes from its airspace and the UK denied use of the Diego Garcia base, while Merz publicly criticized the war.
insights.speaker: Marina Henke, Iren Marinova, and Till Knobloch
insights.so_what: Allied basing and overflight can no longer be assumed by Washington, a concrete constraint on US power projection.
insights.quote: Spain would not allow U.S. warplanes access to its airspace, and the United Kingdom would not allow U.S. forces to use the Diego Garcia base
insights.locator: ACROSS THE WATER / 'Although the Trump administration might applaud'
